evidence_quotes: Manipulative design tricks are still everywhere.
Features such as loot boxes, autoplay, endless scrolling and other addictive loops are designed to maximise usage and spending, not to serve the user.
Auto-renewals and free trials that turn into subscriptions without proper notice also remain common.
Drip pricing (showing a low price up front and piling on charges at checkout) is still widespread.
Influencers and platforms often blur the line between genuine content and paid promotion.
Clear and consistent disclosure should be mandatory, with responsibility shared between both the influencer and the platform.
Protecting what people already own If someone buys a game or app, they shouldnt lose access just because a company decides to pull it from its store or shut down its servers.
Consumers deserve lasting rights to what theyve paid for.

paragraphs.text: 1. On the word sideloading When I install software on my computer, nobody calls it sideloading. Its just installing software. Yet on phones, companies like Apple and Google have popularised the term sideloading to make the exact same thing sound dangerous or illegitimate. This is misleading framing. Installing an app from outside the official store should be recognised as a normal and legitimate action, just like installing programs on a PC. The law should avoid reinforcing biased language that helps big companies control the narrative. 2. Freedom to install apps Users should be free to install apps on the devices they own without unnecessary hurdles. Requiring developer verification or creating friction in the process hurts small developers, hobbyists, and open-source projects. It also undermines Europes digital independence. Rules should support genuine openness and competition instead of strengthening gatekeepers. 3. Dark patterns Manipulative design tricks are still everywhere. Things like: i. fake countdown timers, ii. making cancellation much harder than sign-up, iii. adding hidden fees at the last moment, iv. or using guilt-tripping wording like No, I dont want to save money. These are all common practices. The Act should ban them clearly and directly. Consumers should never need ten clicks to unsubscribe when they only needed one to subscribe. 4. Addictive and exploitative design Features such as loot boxes, autoplay, endless scrolling and other addictive loops are designed to maximise usage and spending, not to serve the user. These are especially harmful for minors. They should be restricted, or at least clearly labelled and easy to turn off. 5. Honest pricing and contracts Drip pricing (showing a low price up front and piling on charges at checkout) is still widespread. Auto-renewals and free trials that turn into subscriptions without proper notice also remain common. The law should ensure full price transparency and make cancellation simple and visible. 6. Influencer marketing Influencers and platforms often blur the line between genuine content and paid promotion. Many consumers, especially younger ones, dont realise theyre watching an advert. Clear and consistent disclosure should be mandatory, with responsibility shared between both the influencer and the platform. 7. Protecting what people already own If someone buys a game or app, they shouldnt lose access just because a company decides to pull it from its store or shut down its servers. Consumers deserve lasting rights to what theyve paid for. 8. Harmonisation across the EU At the moment, different Member States enforce different standards, which creates confusion for consumers and developers. The Act should bring clear, harmonised rules so protections apply equally across the EU. --- In short 1. Stop calling normal app installation sideloading. 2. Guarantee the right to freely install apps. 3. Ban dark patterns and manipulative design. 4. Make cancellation as easy as sign-up. 5. Restrict addictive design, especially for children. 6. Ensure honest pricing and contracts. 7. Make influencer marketing transparent. 8. Protect digital ownership. 9. Harmonise rules across all EU countries.
